refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain teleoperation communication channels with end-to-end latency (sensor-to-operator-to-actuator) not exceeding 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions, enabling safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is vague regarding measurement points and statistical bounds. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured end-to-end (full round-trip through sensing, transmission, processing, and actuation), specifies the p95 percentile as the acceptance criterion, and anchors to 'agreed network conditions' to establish testable boundary conditions. This supports the high safety criticality by making the requirement verifiable and traceable.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrument teleoperation path with timestamped events at sensor acquisition, network ingress/egress, operator interface input, and actuator command execution. Collect latency samples across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) and compute p95 latency distribution. Validate against 150 ms threshold under acceptance test scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-TELE-001: Acquire and transmit equipment sensor data (position, orientation, load, environmental) to remote operator with minimal latency contribution.
FR-TELE-002: Receive and execute operator control commands (steering, lift, bucket, emergency stop) with minimal latency contribution.
FR-TELE-003: Manage network transmission and reception to minimize jitter and packet loss under agreed network conditions.
FR-TELE-004: Monitor and report teleoperation latency status to operator and safety systems in real-time.
FR-TELE-005: Gracefully degrade or halt teleoperation if latency exceeds safe thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency sensor-to-network pipeline with hardware timestamping, prioritized network queuing, and optimized serialization; pair with operator-side command buffering and predictive rendering to mask network jitter.
DP-B: Deploy edge computing node co-located with equipment to pre-process sensor data and buffer commands, reducing end-to-end latency by offloading computation from remote operator station.

DP-C: Establish dedicated, QoS-managed network link (5G, private LTE, or wired) with latency SLA guarantees; implement adaptive codec and frame-rate selection to stay within latency budget under varying conditions.

DP-D: Combine hardware-accelerated sensor acquisition (FPGA/DSP), edge pre-processing, and QoS-managed network with operator-side predictive UI to achieve sub-150ms p95 latency through redundant, low-latency pathways.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Combine hardware-accelerated sensor acquisition (FPGA/DSP), edge pre-processing, and QoS-managed network with operator-side predictive UI to achieve sub-150ms p95 latency through redundant, low-latency pathways.

potential_sources: DP-D couples sensor hardware design (FPGA/DSP selection) to network architecture (QoS management) and operator UI (predictive rendering). This is acceptable because all three are necessary to meet the 150 ms p95 requirement; decoupling any one would likely violate the latency constraint.

DP-D does not create information hiding violations: sensor acquisition, edge processing, network management, and operator UI are logically distinct functions with well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link).
coupling_check.notes: The coupling is functional and necessary, not arbitrary. Each sub-component (hardware acceleration, edge processing, QoS network, predictive UI) addresses a distinct latency source. Decoupling would require relaxing the 150 ms requirement or accepting higher risk.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: 'Agreed network conditions' are defined and stable (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, <50 ms backbone latency, <5% packet loss). Risk: If actual field conditions deviate, latency may exceed 150 ms. Mitigation: Establish network SLA and conduct field validation.

Assumption: FPGA/DSP hardware is available and can be integrated into equipment without cost/schedule impact. Risk: Hardware lead times or integration complexity may delay deployment. Mitigation: Early procurement and prototype validation.

Assumption: Operator training and UI design support predictive operation (operator accepts slight latency in exchange for predictive feedback). Risk: Operator may distrust predictive rendering and attempt to compensate, increasing latency perception. Mitigation: Human factors testing and operator feedback loops.

Assumption: Edge pre-processing does not introduce additional latency (e.g., buffering, serialization). Risk: Poorly designed edge pipeline could add 20–50 ms. Mitigation: Profile edge processing and optimize critical path.
Risk: Redundant pathways increase system complexity and potential failure modes. Mitigation: Implement health monitoring and graceful fallback to primary path.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) hardware acceleration target (e.g., FPGA for sensor timestamping, <5 ms latency), (2) edge processing budget (e.g., <20 ms for data fusion and compression), (3) network SLA (e.g., 5G with <50 ms RTT), (4) operator UI latency budget (e.g., <30 ms for predictive rendering). This decomposition ensures each component is sized to contribute proportionally to the 150 ms budget.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all hardware, firmware, and software components responsible for acquiring sensor data from the Vehicle, transmitting it over the Network Link, receiving operator commands via Tasking, and executing those commands on the Vehicle. Includes sensor drivers, edge processing logic, network protocol stack, operator UI, and latency monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Sensor data stream (position, orientation, load, environmental) from Vehicle to Teleoperation module, then to remote operator. Latency-critical; must support high-frequency updates (e.g., 50–100 Hz) with minimal buffering.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator control commands (steering, lift, bucket, emergency stop) received by Teleoperation module and forwarded to Vehicle for execution. Latency-critical; must support low-latency command injection and acknowledgment.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication channel (5G, LTE, or wired) managed by Teleoperation module. Responsible for QoS management, adaptive codec selection, and latency monitoring to ensure p95 ≤ 150 ms.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Real-time latency status and degradation alerts sent from Teleoperation module to Safety Services. Enables safety system to halt teleoperation if latency exceeds safe thresholds.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural and intended home for all latency-critical teleoperation logic. Sensor acquisition, network management, command execution, and latency monitoring are core responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert interface extends to Safety Services for safety-critical monitoring.

Vehicle module owns the physical equipment and actuators, but latency-critical network and operator UI logic should not reside here. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would scatter teleoperation logic across two modules, violating cohesion and increasing diffusion risk.

Safety Services owns safety policy and monitoring, but should not own the teleoperation latency control logic itself. Safety Services should consume SafetyAlert from Teleoperation and enforce safety policies (e.g., halt if latency exceeds threshold), not implement the latency optimization.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-D here would bypass the Teleoperation module and scatter implementation across multiple sub-modules, violating architectural layering and increasing integration complexity.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the correct and sufficient boundary for implementing DP-D. It owns the full latency-critical path: sensor acquisition, edge processing, network management, operator UI, and latency monitoring. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and minimal, reducing coupling to Vehicle and Safety Services.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become large and complex if it also owns operator UI rendering and predictive algorithms. Mitigation: Decompose Teleoperation into sub-modules (e.g., SensorAcquisition, EdgeProcessing, NetworkManager, OperatorUI) at the next level, ensuring each sub-module has a single responsibility.

Risk: Network Link interface may be shared with other systems (e.g., diagnostics, firmware updates), creating contention and latency spikes. Mitigation: Implement QoS prioritization and bandwidth reservation for teleoperation traffic.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface couples Teleoperation to Safety Services. If Safety Services is slow to respond to latency alerts, teleoperation may continue beyond safe thresholds. Mitigation: Implement local latency thresholds in Teleoperation module with automatic fallback (e.g., switch to autonomous mode or halt).

Risk: Edge pre-processing logic may be duplicated if Vehicle module also performs similar processing. Mitigation: Establish clear ownership: Teleoperation owns latency-critical pre-processing; Vehicle owns equipment-specific control logic.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module owns the full latency budget (150 ms) and is responsible for allocating sub-budgets to sensor acquisition (<5 ms), edge processing (<20 ms), network transmission (<50 ms), operator UI (<30 ms), and safety monitoring (<15 ms). Document these allocations in the DP and ensure each sub-module is sized accordingly. Establish a latency monitoring and reporting interface (SafetyAlert) to enable Safety Services to enforce safety policies without owning the teleoperation logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D directly addresses the refined FR by combining hardware acceleration, edge processing, QoS-managed network, and predictive UI to achieve sub-150ms p95 latency. The DP is specific, measurable, and traceable to the FR. No information hiding violations detected; the coupling is functional and necessary.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module owns the full latency-critical path, but SafetyAlert interface extends to Safety Services. This is acceptable because Safety Services is a consumer of latency status, not an owner of latency control logic. Diffusion score is low (5%).
Network Link interface is shared with other systems (diagnostics, firmware updates). Mitigation: Implement QoS prioritization to ensure teleoperation traffic is not starved by lower-priority traffic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the natural and sufficient boundary for DP-D. Sensor acquisition, edge processing, network management, operator UI, and latency monitoring are cohesive and belong together. External interfaces are minimal and well-defined. No alternative module assignment is recommended.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is verifiable: latency can be measured end-to-end using timestamped events at sensor acquisition, network ingress/egress, operator input, and actuator execution. p95 latency can be computed from a sample of measurements. The 150 ms threshold is testable under agreed network conditions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of DP-D to Teleoperation module is clear and unambiguous. Teleoperation owns the full latency-critical path and is responsible for meeting the 150 ms p95 requirement. Sub-allocations (sensor acquisition, edge processing, network, operator UI) will be refined at the next decomposition level.

recommended_revisions: Establish and document 'agreed network conditions' (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, <50 ms backbone latency, <5% packet loss) as a constraint. This is critical for verifying the FR in the field.

Define latency budget allocation across sub-components: sensor acquisition (<5 ms), edge processing (<20 ms), network transmission (<50 ms), operator UI (<30 ms), safety monitoring (<15 ms). This ensures each sub-module is sized proportionally.
Specify hardware acceleration targets (e.g., FPGA for sensor timestamping) and network SLA requirements (e.g., 5G with <50 ms RTT) in the DP to reduce ambiguity.
Establish latency monitoring and reporting interface (SafetyAlert) to enable Safety Services to enforce safety policies without owning teleoperation logic.
Plan for field validation under actual network conditions to confirm that DP-D achieves the 150 ms p95 requirement. Include contingency plans if field conditions deviate from agreed assumptions.

decision.justification: The refined FR is clear, verifiable, and traceable. DP-D is specific and addresses the FR without information hiding violations. Teleoperation module is the correct and sufficient boundary for implementation. However, DP-D is not atomic; it comprises multiple sub-components (hardware acceleration, edge processing, QoS network, predictive UI) that require further decomposition to assign ownership, define interfaces, and allocate latency budgets. Proceeding to the next level will refine the architecture and reduce implementation risk.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-TELE-001: Acquire and transmit equipment sensor data (position, orientation, load, environmental) to remote operator with minimal latency contribution (<5 ms).
FR-TELE-002: Receive and execute operator control commands (steering, lift, bucket, emergency stop) with minimal latency contribution (<5 ms).
FR-TELE-003: Manage network transmission and reception to minimize jitter and packet loss under agreed network conditions (<50 ms RTT).
FR-TELE-004: Monitor and report teleoperation latency status to operator and safety systems in real-time (<15 ms).
FR-TELE-005: Gracefully degrade or halt teleoperation if latency exceeds safe thresholds.

assumptions: Agreed network conditions are defined and stable (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, <50 ms backbone latency, <5% packet loss).
FPGA/DSP hardware is available and can be integrated into equipment without cost/schedule impact.
Operator training and UI design support predictive operation.
Edge pre-processing does not introduce additional latency (e.g., buffering, serialization).
Teleoperation module owns the full latency budget (150 ms) and is responsible for allocating sub-budgets to sub-components.
open_risks: Field conditions may deviate from agreed network conditions, causing latency to exceed 150 ms. Mitigation: Establish network SLA and conduct field validation.
Hardware lead times or integration complexity may delay deployment. Mitigation: Early procurement and prototype validation.
Operator may distrust predictive rendering and attempt to compensate, increasing latency perception. Mitigation: Human factors testing and operator feedback loops.
Edge pre-processing may introduce unexpected latency (20–50 ms). Mitigation: Profile edge processing and optimize critical path.
Redundant pathways increase system complexity and potential failure modes. Mitigation: Implement health monitoring and graceful fallback.
Network Link interface may be shared with other systems, creating contention and latency spikes. Mitigation: Implement QoS prioritization and bandwidth reservation.

Safety Services may be slow to respond to latency alerts, allowing teleoperation to continue beyond safe thresholds. Mitigation: Implement local latency thresholds in Teleoperation module with automatic fallback.
evidence_needed: Network SLA documentation specifying agreed conditions (bandwidth, latency, jitter, packet loss).
Hardware acceleration specifications (FPGA/DSP latency targets, integration feasibility).
Edge processing latency profile (sensor data fusion, compression, serialization).
Operator UI latency budget and predictive rendering algorithm design.
Field validation results confirming p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions.
Safety policy documentation specifying latency thresholds and fallback actions.
Operator training and human factors testing results.
